explicit_text: I was born and raised in Chapel Hill, North Carolina, specifically on Hillsborough Street, an area we called Windy Hill. Growing up there in the 1940s and 1950s, the community was unique because people of similar economic status lived together, including poor white neighbors who were supportive and friendly. Chapel Hill had its challenges, including racial tensions and KKK threats, but it was also a place where the black community was strong, with solid schools, churches, and community centers. I attended local schools and participated in sports and community activities, which shaped much of my early life.
implicit_text: I was born and raised in a college town in North Carolina, specifically on Hillsborough Street, an area we called Windy Hill. Growing up there in the 1940s and 1950s, the community was unique because people of similar economic status lived together, including poor white neighbors who were supportive and friendly. This town had its challenges, including racial tensions and KKK threats, but it was also a place where the black community was strong, with solid schools, churches, and community centers. I attended local schools and participated in sports and community activities, which shaped much of my early life.

explicit_text: Windy Hill was the name we black folks used for the part of Hillsborough Street where I was born and grew up. It was a small segment of black homeowners surrounded by a white community, so to enter or leave Windy Hill, you had to pass through white neighborhoods. Despite the segregation, we had strong bonds within Windy Hill and with some of the poor white neighbors, who were often friends and supportive. However, the area was not without hostility, as KKK threats were sadly common during my childhood.

explicit_text: I attended Northside Elementary School for my early education, where we sometimes had to attend in shifts because there were so many students. Despite the half-day schedules, the teachers were dedicated and had high expectations for us. They maintained close relationships with parents, even making home visits to discuss our progress and set expectations for the school year. This early foundation was important in shaping my attitude toward learning and discipline.

entity: Lincoln High School (Chapel Hill, North Carolina)
explicit_text: After elementary school, I walked about two and a half miles to Lincoln High School on Merritt Mill Road every day, passing by Franklin Street schools on the way. Lincoln was a home away from home for me, led by Principal C.A. McDougle, who was like a surrogate father and disciplinarian, ensuring a strong educational environment despite limited resources. I played football there for three seasons, even captaining the 1961 state championship team, and was part of a band praised statewide under Clark Egerton and Bob Bell. Lincoln was a place of high expectations, community support, and lasting inspiration.

McDougle, who was like a surrogate father and disciplinarian, ensuring a strong educational environment despite limited resources. I played football there for three seasons, even captaining the 1961 state championship team, and was part of a band praised statewide under Clark Egerton and Bob Bell. It was a place of high expectations, community support, and lasting inspiration.

explicit_text: Franklin Street was a significant landmark in my daily life because it was where Chapel Hill High School and an elementary school were located, and I passed by it every day walking to Lincoln High School. Seeing those schools made me wonder if it would be better to attend there instead of walking so far to Lincoln. Franklin Street represented the white schools in town, and it was a reminder of the segregation and inequalities we faced, such as receiving secondhand books from those schools.

explicit_text: Merritt Mill Road was where Lincoln High School was located, and I had to walk about two and a half miles there from my home on Hillsborough Street. It was a daily trek for me and other students, with no school bus service available. Despite the distance and the challenges of the walk, Lincoln became a central part of my life, both academically and athletically.

explicit_text: During my childhood in Chapel Hill, it was not uncommon to see hateful messages from the Ku Klux Klan, like their name written in the middle of the road telling black folks to 'get the hell out.' These acts of intimidation were a harsh reality of the environment we lived in during the 1940s and 1950s. Despite this, our community remained strong and resilient, supported by schools, churches, and neighbors.

Despite this, our community remained strong and resilient, supported by schools, churches, and neighbors.

explicit_text: Clark Egerton was the band director at Lincoln High School during my time there, and under his leadership, along with Bob Bell, our band was considered one of the best in the state. Every time we performed, we received high praise, which was a source of pride for our school and community. His dedication helped create an environment where students like me could excel in music alongside athletics.

The community came out in full support of Lincoln's teams, which were known for their excellence in sports. Playing and winning there was a proud part of my high school years.

entity: North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University
explicit_text: After graduating from Lincoln High School, I attended North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University on a football scholarship. I earned my BS degree there and was involved in significant events like the 1962-63 Woolworth's desegregation boycott. I also played football alongside notable teammates like Jesse Jackson, which was a formative experience during my college years.

explicit_text: I was part of the 1962-63 boycott of Woolworth's, which was a pivotal event in the Civil Rights Movement aimed at desegregating the store. This boycott took place while I was a student at North Carolina A&T, and it was an important moment of activism and change in our community. Participating in this movement showed me the power of collective action for civil rights.

explicit_text: Coach Bradshaw was my football coach during my junior year at Lincoln High School, and he was like a father figure to us. He not only coached us on the field but also encouraged us academically, sometimes taking us out of study hall to work on basketball skills or to motivate us. He was instrumental in helping me secure a football scholarship for college by seeking out opportunities and providing guidance.

explicit_text: Peerman was the football coach who came in during my senior year at Lincoln High School. He was a dynamic leader who, along with Coach Calter from Chapel Hill High, left a lasting impact on our football program. In fact, the Chapel Hill football stadium was later named after both Peerman and Coach Calter, highlighting their importance to the community and the sport.

entity: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill
explicit_text: My father worked as a janitor at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, which was part of our family’s connection to the town. Although I did not attend UNC myself, the university was a significant institution in Chapel Hill and influenced the community environment where I grew up. My parents’ work there reflected the opportunities and limitations for black workers at the time.

explicit_text: C. A. McDougle was the principal of Lincoln High School during my years there, and he was much more than just an administrator—he was like a surrogate father to us. He was a strict disciplinarian who ensured no students hung out in the halls without a reason and was deeply involved in the classroom, sometimes sitting in to oversee teaching quality. Mr. McDougle cared about our well-being both in and out of school and was the driving force behind maintaining a strong, supportive, and effective educational environment despite the challenges we faced.
